428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0856
February 21,2007
Boyd L. Hill
McCormick, Kidman & Behrens,LLp
650 Town Center Dr., Ste 100
Costa Mesa, California 92626-7197
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-013
DearMr. Hill:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jim Snyder, a
board member of the san Gorgoniopass waterAgency, *g*d;;ih" "t"ni". "r
interest provisions of the political Reform act 1tnJ.,ecr1.
QUESTTONS
1. May a board member of the San Gorgonio pass water Agency (..Agency,)
vote on a proposed agreement under which-the Agency would assisithe'city Jre*rirrg
(*city") and the Banning Heights Mutual water dompany (.,comp any,)(collectively
"Buyers') with the purchase of water transport facilities lihe ..Facilities;; rrom southem
california Edison ("scE')? The Buyers provide water service to properties owned by
the board member located several miles from the Facilities.
2' May the board member serye on a committee of the Agency tasked with
reviewing the proposed purchase agreement?
CONCLUSION
I and 2' Yes. Under the facts presented, the board member may participate and
vote on board matters, including the committee, pertaining to trr" propor.J agreement so
long as the specific circumstances listed in regutatio n 18,7"05.21b) are not pr"J*t to rebut
the presumption of no mateiar effect, as discussed berow.
I Government code sections 8 1000-9 1014. commission regulations appear at title 2,sectiorx
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 2
The Agency is a State Water Project contractor with the obligation to provide
water from the State Water Project to the San Gorgonio Pass *"u. Jim Snyder, a member
of the Agency's board, has authorizedyouto request advice on his behalfregarding a
decision of the Agency to enter into an agre"mert (,.Agreement,) with scn La trre
Buyers under which the Buyers would purchase tom Scr its facilities that hansport
water from the Whitewater River to the San Gorgonio River bed (.?aciliti;.;r. The
Buyers already own the rights to this water and operate wells at the San *"r**a River.
Transfer of the Facilities will not change the source, amount or quality of thJwater that
Buyers supply to customers. Itmay result in increased certainty of the existing supply.
As part of the Facilities transfer, SCE will be required to make .o-" r.pul^ io'tt "
Facilities. The proposed agreement contemplat"r th" Agency providing assistance with a
portion of the Buyers' costs and in obtaining necessary p"""id. The Alency is forming a
committee for the pu{pose of reviewing the Agreement.- The board member would like to
serve on this committee.
The board member's residence is located on one of five lots he owns in the
unincorporated area of Banning Bend. The Company, anonprofit mufual water company
in which property owners are not only water p*"t ** 
but are shareholders of the
Company, provides water to properties in Banning Bend. only three of the five lots have
water service connections. The Riverside CountlHealth Departrnent has prohibited
additional connections until a second source of water is deveioped. The contemplated
transfer of the Facilities does not provide an additional source of water. The board
member also owns residential property in the City which he rents to his granddaughter at
the cost of his mortgage and insurance payments. In our conversation oiFebruary 9,
2007, you advised thlt this property has never been rented to anyone else and that it was
specificallypurchased for the granddaughter's use. Water for this p-p"rty;; purchased
from the City. The board member's properties are located several miles from the
Facilities.
rACTS
ANALYSIS
Your question implicates the Act's conflict of interest provisions which ensure
that public officials "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported
them'" (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 pro6iUit. anypublic offi"iul fro*
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his oi her official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financiallnterest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have amaterial financial effect on one or more of the pubt[ official,s economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The commission has adopted a standard eight-step


File No. 4-07-013
Page No. 3
analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a
glven govemmental decision. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(S).) -
l- & 2. Is the board member a "public official" and will he be making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes "eYe,ryt' member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
goveflrment agency . . .." (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a)(l).) As a board member
of the Agency, he is a public official subject to the Act's conflicrof interest provisions.
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official , acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.t The board member
will "make a goveflrmental decision" ifhe votes on the Agreement that would obligate
the Agency to pay for some of the Buyer's costs ofpurctrasing the Facilities.
If the board member engages in negotiations without significant substantive
review, provides advice or makes recommendations, he will be-..participating,, in the
decision (regulation 18702.2). He will be'tnfluencing" that decision iihe uip"*, before
or otherwise attempts to influence any mernber, officer, employee or consultlirt of the
Agency. (Regulation18702.3.) Thus, by serving on the committee that will review the
Agreement, the board member would be participating in making and influencing a
governmental decision.
3. What are the board memberrs economic interests _ the possible sources
of a conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial efflect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a mernber of his or her immediate family, or on any of th" official,s economic
interests, described as follows:
a
an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section t7r03(a); regulation 1g703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, pafiner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 I 03 (d); regulation 1 3703. I (b));
an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section s7103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
a


File No. A-07-0t3
Page No. 4
o aneconomicinterest inany sourceofincome,includingpromisedincome, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 I 03 (c); regulation I 87 03.3);
o an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the grfts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103ie); relilation
18703.4.)
In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this isthe ..personal
financial effects" rule. (Section g7103; regulation 1g703.5.)
Rental Business:
The property the board member rents out may be considered to be a real estate
business owned by him. If the board member has aninvestment of $2,000 or more in this
business, he has an economic interest in the business. (Section g7103(a); ."grrtutio,
18703.1(a).) Howeveq section 82005 provides that "business entig/, means any
otganization or enterprise operated for profit. including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint v-enture, syndicate, corporation or
association. (Emphasis added.) Under the particular circumstances you have described
in which the property was purchased to provide for his granddaughter and she pays the
equivalent of the mortgage and insurance payments, we conclude that he is not operating
the rental property for profit.
Ownership Interest tn the Company:
You indicate that the Company is a non-pro fit organizatron. Accordingly, the
board member's interest in the company,s shares is not ir, ""oro-i"i;";;1" 
u
business entity because, as stated above, section 82005 defines ..business entity', so as to
only include entities operated for profit. (Christensen Adviceletter No. l,-gi-sza.l
Real Property:
The board l1mber owns six properties in which he has an interest of $2,000 or
more' These include his residence, the four unimproved lots in Banning Bench and the
residential property occupied by his granddaught"r in th" city.
Source of Income:
Because the board member receives rent from his granddaughter, she is
considered to be a source of income to him if she pays hiri $500 oimore within l2


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 5
months prior to his making a govemmental decision. Presumably, she pays him $500 or
more per year. "rncome" is defined in section 82030 as including .,rentl, without any
allowance for offsetting expenses.
Personal Finances
The board member also has an economic interest in his personal finances and
those of his immediate family. (Section 87|}3;regulation 18703.5.) A governmental
decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decisionwill-result in the
personal expenses, income, assets orliabilities of an official orhis orherimmediate
-fr-ilv increasing or decreasing. Qbid.) Your letter does not mention any impact on the
!'oard member's personal finances as the result of the upcoming decisions; therefore we
do not further analyze this issue. However, please note that a conflict of interest may
arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the Agreanent will have an
effect on his personal finances.
4. Are the board member's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2 sets forth the circumstances under which real property is
considered directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or anypart of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundmies (or the proposed boundaries)
of the property wfrich is the subject of the govemmentA aecision. for prrpor",
of subdivision (a)(5), real property is locatid 'within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision' if any partof the real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the r"d"r"lop-ent prdect area.
"(2) T},e governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusionln or exclusi,on from any city,
county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision uif""tirg the ieal'property.
For purposes of this subdivision, the terms 'zoring'and 'rezoiing, shalirefer to
the act of establishiag or changing the zoning or land use designa:tion on the real
property in which the official has an interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 6
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of
,fr 
tax.es or fees assessed or imposed on the r"ui p.opoiy in which the official
has an rnterest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, toselect the
project atea, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to
cettify the environmental document, to adopt the redeveiopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend *y oith" above
decisions; *9 ryl property in which the official has an i"to.t, oiany part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundari"g oiflr"
redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves "o5ry"I?n of, or improvements to, steets, water,
sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
The decision to enter into an agreement to spend Agency funds to assist with the
cost of transferring the Facilities does not fall within any of the circumstances listed
above' The Facilities are located on land that is well beyond 500 r"et oiuv ortn" board
member's properties. The decision does not involve ,o*ng,annexation, sale, purchase,
lease, inclusion transfeE or subdivision of the board membei,s r"ut p.op"rty.- The
decision does not involve taxes or fees affecting his real property, nor does it involve
survey areas, project areas, preliminaryplans, environmenta ao"".*ir, o.
redevelopment plalg-or ar9as. Finally, inasmuch as the agreement merely involves the
hansfer of the Facilities, obligates SCE only to make certaln repairs i, "orr""tion with
the transfer, and will not result in any change to the.o*"", amount or quality of water
supplies, the decision to enter into the Agreiment does not involve..construJtion o{, or
improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.,, The board
member's real property is, therefore, indirectly invived in the a""irlo.ri 
'
Source of Income:
Regulation 18704.1provides that"aperson, including business entities, sources
of income. and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
' Yot' raise the issue that even if the board member's real propert5r were to be deemed directly
involved in the govemmental decision under the above criteria, the e?eption of subdivision (b)(2) would
apply because SCE's responsibility under the Agreement is limited to repairs and not improvements.
Subdivision (b)(2) provides that notwithstanding a public official's economic interest being directly
involved in a governmental decision, it is deemed indirectly involveJif th" a""irioo rot"ly-"il""*,
repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storrn drainage or similar facilities.
we need not rely on this exception because *" hur" aeierminea that irnder reguh#" I gr;;.iia) ttre board
member's real property is indirectly involved. The board member should, however, be alert to future board
decisions which may involve construction of, or improvements to, the Facilities, in which 
"ur" hi, property
would be directly involved under regulation 1g704.2(a)(6).


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 7
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (l) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an'appliJation, ciaim, appeal,or similar
request; or Q) Is a named party ir, 9I is the subject of the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's ageitcy:' (Emphasis aao!a.; since the board
member's granddaughter, a source of income to him, is nol initiating proceedings
regarding the Facilities or the AgreynTt nor is she a party in or theiub;ect of any such
proceedings, this economic interest is deemed to be indirectly involved in the
governmental deci sion.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard.
A conflictof interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefllmental decision on a public official's economic interestsls material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards aply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending o, it ".ruto." of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly oi indlir"ctly"irrotrr"Jin the
agency's decision.
Real Property:
Regulation 18705.2(b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for the
board member's real property interests. tt provides that the..financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the
govefirmental decision is presumed not to be material. This pr"r,r*pti* maybe
rebutted by proof thlt there are specific circumstan"", ,rg*ding the govenilental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the reu ffierty in which the
public has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have amateial financial effect on the real p'roperty in which the
public ofiEcial has an interest." Examples of these specihc circumstances include
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an ""oro*i" interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(c) The chalactgr of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,iitensity ofuse,
noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of tire neighborhooi.,,
Inasmuch as.jh9_nroPosed Agreement will not change the source, amount or
quality of water avallable to the board member,s propertiei it does ,ot upp"* it "


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 8
presumption is rebutted. However, we af,e not in a position to make decisions as to
whether there is enough "proof'of specific circumitances to rebut the presumption.
Source of Income:
Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to the board member. For indirectly involved sources
of income, the financial effect of a governmental decision is deerned material with
respect to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any oftrre following
applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (otherihan reut proi"rty;
by $ 1,000 ormore; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, Cahlornia Code of
Regulations, section r g7 05.2(b). " (Reguration t bzos.: (bx3).)
The only source of income you identified is the board mernber's granddaughter. While
she may have a real property interest in the house she occupies, pirsuant tJsubdivision
(B) and regulation 18705.2b), it would only be considered indirectly invotrrJ in the
decision- As stated.above, underreguration rg7o5.2(b),,,thefinancial effect of a
govemmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in ttre glvernmentat
decision is presumed not to be material." The circumstantes under which the
presumption may be rebutted are listed in.subsection (b)(1) for interests in real property
other than leaseholds and in subsection (bX2) ror teasetroli interests. None upp"*
applicable to the facts you have provided and therefore the presumption would appear to
stand.
6' rs it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon the board memberrs economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? @oes a conflict of interest result?)
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the mateiality standards appiicable io that
economic interest will be met as a result of the gor"**"ntal decisi#. qneguiation
18706(a)') A financial effect need not be certain to be considered r"urorruUt-y foreseeable,
but it must be more than amere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC opr. rqs.l


File No. A-07-013
Page No. 9
Real Property:
The question of whether financial consequences are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a govemmental decision is made must always depend on the facts oie;ch particular
case' (In re Thorner, supra.) You have not advised us of facts that would tend to rebut
the presumption that the governmental decision will not have a material financial effect
on the board member's real property. You state that the transfer of the Facilities ,,may
result in greater certainty" for existing water supplies but .hill not increase the amount or
quality of water avulable" to property owners. The.e facts would awearto support a
conclusion that the presumption should stand.
Source of Income:
It is not clear from your letter whether the board mernber's granddaughter rents
the property month-to-month or if she has a leasehold interest in the property.3 If she is
deemed to have a leasehold interest, then under.egutation 1g705.2(b), the financial effect
of the govemmental decision is presumed not to bJ material. If not, we reach a similar
result because it does not appearthat the standard under regulation 1g705.3 is met.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71.) The determination of wirether oinot it is rJasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the board member,s
economic interests is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately fo, hil il a""ia".
7. The .,Public GenerallyD Exception.
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to take a role in the governmental decision in questior. trtn"-r"urorruuiv roreseeable
matenal financial effect of a governmental decisio, on the public official,s economic
interest is indistinguishabre "from its effect onrhe public g"noully,,, then thafubhc
official does not havS aconflict. (Section g7rl3;regulatilns 1s700(b)(7 ), 1s707(a).)
Under the facts you have provided, it does not appear that the .puuu" i"rrliiliy,
exception applies.a

code section 82033 shall not include the interesi of a tenant t" ";;;;; 
tenancy of
a You indicate the board member owng multiple properties. The ownership of multiple interests
does not automaticallypreclude application of the "public;J"rrily,; exception. However, under your
facts, it would appear that ownership of six residendal lots,"four oirorri"r, are one acre, one of which is five
acres' and two of which are occupied by small residences, would result in a financial ef,[ect on the board
il"#:"#::.is 
not "substantially the same" as the effect ", r.lgr"fi;t segment ofproperry owners or

8. Legal Required participation.
You have not provided any information regarding the application of the final step,
step eight regarding the exception for "legalrequiied paiicipation.,'

Luisa Menchaca

otganization or enterprise operated for profit. including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint v-enture, syndicate, corporation or
association. (Emphasis added.) Under the particular circumstances you have described
in which the property was purchased to provide for his granddaughter and she pays the
equivalent of the mortgage and insurance payments, we conclude that he is not operating
the rental property for profit.
Ownership Interest tn the Company:
You indicate that the Company is a non-pro fit organizatron. Accordingly, the
board member's interest in the company,s shares is not ir, ""oro-i"i;";;1" 
u
business entity because, as stated above, section 82005 defines ..business entity', so as to
only include entities operated for profit. (Christensen Adviceletter No. l,-gi-sza.l
Real Property:
The board l1mber owns six properties in which he has an interest of $2,000 or
more' These include his residence, the four unimproved lots in Banning Bench and the
residential property occupied by his granddaught"r in th" city.
Source of Income:
Because the board member receives rent from his granddaughter, she is
considered to be a source of income to him if she pays hiri $500 oimore within l2
months prior to his making a govemmental decision. Presumably, she pays him $500 or
more per year. "rncome" is defined in section 82030 as including .,rentl, without any
allowance for offsetting expenses.
Personal Finances
The board member also has an economic interest in his personal finances and
those of his immediate family. (Section 87|}3;regulation 18703.5.) A governmental
decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decisionwill-result in the
personal expenses, income, assets orliabilities of an official orhis orherimmediate
-fr-ilv increasing or decreasing. Qbid.) Your letter does not mention any impact on the
!'oard member's personal finances as the result of the upcoming decisions; therefore we
do not further analyze this issue. However, please note that a conflict of interest may
arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the Agreanent will have an
effect on his personal finances.
4. Are the board member's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2 sets forth the circumstances under which real property is
considered directly involved in a governmental decision:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or anypart of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundmies (or the proposed boundaries)
of the property wfrich is the subject of the govemmentA aecision. for prrpor",
of subdivision (a)(5), real property is locatid 'within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision' if any partof the real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the r"d"r"lop-ent prdect area.
"(2) T},e governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusionln or exclusi,on from any city,
county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision uif""tirg the ieal'property.
For purposes of this subdivision, the terms 'zoring'and 'rezoiing, shalirefer to
the act of establishiag or changing the zoning or land use designa:tion on the real
property in which the official has an interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of
,fr 
tax.es or fees assessed or imposed on the r"ui p.opoiy in which the official
has an rnterest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, toselect the
project atea, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to
cettify the environmental document, to adopt the redeveiopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend *y oith" above
decisions; *9 ryl property in which the official has an i"to.t, oiany part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundari"g oiflr"
redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves "o5ry"I?n of, or improvements to, steets, water,
sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
The decision to enter into an agreement to spend Agency funds to assist with the
cost of transferring the Facilities does not fall within any of the circumstances listed
above' The Facilities are located on land that is well beyond 500 r"et oiuv ortn" board
member's properties. The decision does not involve ,o*ng,annexation, sale, purchase,
lease, inclusion transfeE or subdivision of the board membei,s r"ut p.op"rty.- The
decision does not involve taxes or fees affecting his real property, nor does it involve
survey areas, project areas, preliminaryplans, environmenta ao"".*ir, o.
redevelopment plalg-or ar9as. Finally, inasmuch as the agreement merely involves the
hansfer of the Facilities, obligates SCE only to make certaln repairs i, "orr""tion with
the transfer, and will not result in any change to the.o*"", amount or quality of water
supplies, the decision to enter into the Agreiment does not involve..construJtion o{, or
improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.,, The board
member's real property is, therefore, indirectly invived in the a""irlo.ri 
'
Source of Income:
Regulation 18704.1provides that"aperson, including business entities, sources
of income. and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
' Yot' raise the issue that even if the board member's real propert5r were to be deemed directly
involved in the govemmental decision under the above criteria, the e?eption of subdivision (b)(2) would
apply because SCE's responsibility under the Agreement is limited to repairs and not improvements.
Subdivision (b)(2) provides that notwithstanding a public official's economic interest being directly
involved in a governmental decision, it is deemed indirectly involveJif th" a""irioo rot"ly-"il""*,
repairs, replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storrn drainage or similar facilities.
we need not rely on this exception because *" hur" aeierminea that irnder reguh#" I gr;;.iia) ttre board
member's real property is indirectly involved. The board member should, however, be alert to future board
decisions which may involve construction of, or improvements to, the Facilities, in which 
"ur" hi, property
would be directly involved under regulation 1g704.2(a)(6).
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (l) Initiates the proceeding in
which the decision will be made by filing an'appliJation, ciaim, appeal,or similar
request; or Q) Is a named party ir, 9I is the subject of the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's ageitcy:' (Emphasis aao!a.; since the board
member's granddaughter, a source of income to him, is nol initiating proceedings
regarding the Facilities or the AgreynTt nor is she a party in or theiub;ect of any such
proceedings, this economic interest is deemed to be indirectly involved in the
governmental deci sion.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard.
A conflictof interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govefllmental decision on a public official's economic interestsls material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards aply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending o, it ".ruto." of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly oi indlir"ctly"irrotrr"Jin the
agency's decision.
Real Property:
Regulation 18705.2(b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for the
board member's real property interests. tt provides that the..financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the
govefirmental decision is presumed not to be material. This pr"r,r*pti* maybe
rebutted by proof thlt there are specific circumstan"", ,rg*ding the govenilental
decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the reu ffierty in which the
public has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have amateial financial effect on the real p'roperty in which the
public ofiEcial has an interest." Examples of these specihc circumstances include
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an ""oro*i" interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(c) The chalactgr of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,iitensity ofuse,
noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of tire neighborhooi.,,
Inasmuch as.jh9_nroPosed Agreement will not change the source, amount or
quality of water avallable to the board member,s propertiei it does ,ot upp"* it "
presumption is rebutted. However, we af,e not in a position to make decisions as to
whether there is enough "proof'of specific circumitances to rebut the presumption.
Source of Income:
Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to the board member. For indirectly involved sources
of income, the financial effect of a governmental decision is deerned material with
respect to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any oftrre following
applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (otherihan reut proi"rty;
by $ 1,000 ormore; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, Cahlornia Code of
Regulations, section r g7 05.2(b). " (Reguration t bzos.: (bx3).)
The only source of income you identified is the board mernber's granddaughter. While
she may have a real property interest in the house she occupies, pirsuant tJsubdivision
(B) and regulation 18705.2b), it would only be considered indirectly invotrrJ in the
decision- As stated.above, underreguration rg7o5.2(b),,,thefinancial effect of a
govemmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in ttre glvernmentat
decision is presumed not to be material." The circumstantes under which the
presumption may be rebutted are listed in.subsection (b)(1) for interests in real property
other than leaseholds and in subsection (bX2) ror teasetroli interests. None upp"*
applicable to the facts you have provided and therefore the presumption would appear to
stand.
6' rs it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon the board memberrs economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? @oes a conflict of interest result?)
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the mateiality standards appiicable io that
economic interest will be met as a result of the gor"**"ntal decisi#. qneguiation
18706(a)') A financial effect need not be certain to be considered r"urorruUt-y foreseeable,
but it must be more than amere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC opr. rqs.l
Real Property:
The question of whether financial consequences are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a govemmental decision is made must always depend on the facts oie;ch particular
case' (In re Thorner, supra.) You have not advised us of facts that would tend to rebut
the presumption that the governmental decision will not have a material financial effect
on the board member's real property. You state that the transfer of the Facilities ,,may
result in greater certainty" for existing water supplies but .hill not increase the amount or
quality of water avulable" to property owners. The.e facts would awearto support a
conclusion that the presumption should stand.
Source of Income:
It is not clear from your letter whether the board mernber's granddaughter rents
the property month-to-month or if she has a leasehold interest in the property.3 If she is
deemed to have a leasehold interest, then under.egutation 1g705.2(b), the financial effect
of the govemmental decision is presumed not to bJ material. If not, we reach a similar
result because it does not appearthat the standard under regulation 1g705.3 is met.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71.) The determination of wirether oinot it is rJasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the board member,s
economic interests is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately fo, hil il a""ia".
7. The .,Public GenerallyD Exception.
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to take a role in the governmental decision in questior. trtn"-r"urorruuiv roreseeable
